_scope_note: Captures the hyper bands (apparent-velocity model), translation mechanics, and the hyper-limit table. DEFERRED for now (per plan): gravity waves, the Tellerman wave, grav-wave sail riding, and dimensional shear beyond the basic hyper-limit rule.

apparent_velocity_model.source: David Weber, 'Effective Speed By Hyper Band' (posted 2009-06-03), collected by Joe Buckley.
apparent_velocity_model.copyright: The source chart is marked 'Copyright (c) 2009-21 Word of Weber, LLC - All Rights Reserved'. Used here as factual canon for a non-commercial fan simulation (see data/README.md). Authorization to be sought at simulator v1.0.0+.
apparent_velocity_model.validation: Reproduces the in-book datapoints: Epsilon 1442c (merchant 0.5c x 2884), Zeta ~2500c (0.7c x 3589 = 2512c), Theta 3000c/2500c (warship/merchant). The low-Delta book figure of 912c is the band's lower end; the chart's representative Delta merchant is 0.5c x 2178 = 1089c.
apparent_velocity_model.note: The two 'Effective Times C' columns in Weber's chart are velocity_multiplier x 0.6 (warship) and x 0.5 (merchant). Band-climb time and the translation bleed-off are recorded below but treated as noise for travel-time purposes (v1): only the 0.3c alpha-translation limit is enforced; a ship cruises at its max band's numbers.

bands.name: Alpha
bands.typical_users: The main/lowest travel band; historically the only one used until the hyper log improved.
bands.danger: Entry above 0.3c is instantly fatal - a limit that took ~200 years to be accepted.

bands.name: Gamma
bands.typical_users: A low travel band. Grayson hyper-generator tech was limited to the middle Gamma band before it joined the Manticoran Alliance.

canon_datapoints.note: Older Manticoran merchant ships, lower END of Delta (below the chart's representative 1089c).
Chart merchant figure (0.5c x 2884 = 1442c); few merchant ships go this fast.

bands.name: Zeta

canon_datapoints.note: Faster freighters / passenger liners at 0.7c (0.7 x 3589 = 2512c ~ 2500c).

bands.typical_users: Frequently used by warships - good speed with moderate safety.

bands.name: Theta
bands.typical_users: The fastest band in normal use; couriers ride its 'ragged edge'.
bands.danger: Highest band in routine use and the most dangerous to travel.

bands.name: Iota
bands.typical_users: Marked 'currently unattainable' on Weber's chart; reachable only with the streak drive (early 20th Century PD), after this sim's 1890 PD epoch.

bands.name: Kappa
bands.typical_users: Highest known band; only reachable with the streak drive (early 20th Century PD). Beyond Weber's chart (which stops at Iota) - multiplier left unset.

translate_up.description: Crossing UP a band boundary bleeds off a percentage of relative velocity (regained by re-accelerating once in the new band; see each band's translation_bleed_off_pct) and crosses dangerous gravity-wave surges at the boundary. The Warshawski sail addresses grav-wave SAILING and shear safety; it does not generally negate the band-translation speed bleed-off for ordinary impeller travel. (The Fandom wiki's 'negated both' wording is grav-wave-specific.)
translate_up.speed_bleed: per-band percentage, from Weber's chart (Alpha 92% down to Iota 48%)

translate_down.description: Each downward translation slows the ship to one-fifth of its relative velocity. Hardware faces no shear danger going down, but crew suffer nausea/distress that worsens with speed.
translate_down.worked_example: A ship at ~0.3c (90,000 km/s) translating down four bands (Delta -> n-space) ends at 90,000 / 5^4 = 144 km/s without re-acceleration.

crash_translation.description: A high-velocity translation: faster but induces strong nausea/physical distress; naval crews train for it, but it is avoided unless necessary. Merchants prefer low-velocity translations to reduce alpha-node wear and crew distress. Modelled later as a 'tactical' flight-plan option; v1 uses the non-crash translation velocity (0.2c).

max_velocity_c.notes: NOT stated on the hyperspace/hyper-limit pages. ~0.8c is the widely-cited particle-shielding ceiling for impeller-wedge ships; the simulator's existing examples use 0.6c for a merchant. Confirm against a primary source before treating as canon. Typical real velocities seen in canon hyper travel are 0.5-0.7c.

hyper_limit.source: David Weber, 'Hyper Limits by stellar spectral class' (posted 2009-06-03; archived at web.archive.org/web/20210507094259/https://davidweber.net/posts/16-hyperlimits-by-class.html), collected by Joe Buckley. Marked 'Copyright (c) 2009-21 Words of Weber, LLC - All Rights Reserved' (see data/README.md). Also UHH.
hyper_limit.description: Radius around a star inside which hyper translation causes fatal gravimetric shear. A ship must be BEYOND this limit to translate into or out of hyperspace. This sets the in-system distance a ship must cross under impeller drive before it can hyper out (departure leg) and where it re-enters n-space inbound.
hyper_limit.notes: Values transcribed verbatim from Weber's authoritative table. F0 = 26.42 (not the 25.42 in earlier captures) restores the monotonic hot->cool decline; the post marks F0 with '* Confirmed typographical entry in More Than Honor', i.e. the book's printed figure was a typo and 26.42 is canon. F5 = 24.20 fits the steady ~0.44 lm/subclass cadence. No remaining anomalies.
hyper_limit.interpolation_note: O, B, and A are given as single representative values (no subclasses). For an O/B/A subclass, interpolate linearly toward the next listed anchor (A -> F0). White dwarfs, giants (other than the one 'Red Giant' entry), and supergiants are not tabulated; invent as needed and flag canon:false. F/G/K/M subclasses are fully tabulated and need no interpolation.

applied_examples._note: Looked up from by_spectral_class for our built systems (and Sol). The lookup is canon-exact, so these are canon values, not fabrications.
